title: Conversions and numeric boundaries

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Conversions and numeric boundaries; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C applies integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions before many operations, then assignment or casts can convert the result to a destination type.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Comparing negative int -1 with unsigned int 1 can convert the negative operand to a large unsigned value and reverse a beginner's expected ordering. Your goal is to predict and test conversion results while guarding values that cannot be represented. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Translation unit and main; pf state expressions; Operators and precedence. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C applies integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions before many operations, then assignment or casts can convert the result to a destination type. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Signed-to-unsigned conversion is modulo the unsigned range, narrowing can lose information, and out-of-range floating or pointer conversions have separate rules. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: The destination variable's type controls how the entire right-hand expression is evaluated from the beginning.

includes: Reason about promotions, signedness, narrowing, overflow and floating-point approximation.
Predict and test conversion results while guarding values that cannot be represented.
excludes: Signed-to-unsigned conversion is modulo the unsigned range, narrowing can lose information, and out-of-range floating or pointer conversions have separate rules.

outcomes: Predict and test conversion results while guarding values that cannot be represented.

examples.title: Conversions and numeric boundaries: defined case
examples.situation: Comparing negative int -1 with unsigned int 1 can convert the negative operand to a large unsigned value and reverse a beginner's expected ordering.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Comparing negative int -1 with unsigned int 1 can convert the negative operand to a large unsigned value and reverse a beginner's expected ordering.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Trace integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions first, then apply the final assignment conversion; cast before an operation only when that semantic change is intended.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Trace integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions first, then apply the final assignment conversion; cast before an operation only when that semantic change is intended.

examples.title: Conversions and numeric boundaries: boundary case
examples.situation: Dividing two int operands truncates before assignment to double, while converting one operand to double requests floating division.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Dividing two int operands truncates before assignment to double, while converting one operand to double requests floating division.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Signed-to-unsigned conversion is modulo the unsigned range, narrowing can lose information, and out-of-range floating or pointer conversions have separate rules.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: The destination variable's type controls how the entire right-hand expression is evaluated from the beginning.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Predict and test conversion results while guarding values that cannot be represented.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The destination variable's type controls how the entire right-hand expression is evaluated from the beginning.
misconceptions.correction: Trace integer promotions and usual arithmetic conversions first, then apply the final assignment conversion; cast before an operation only when that semantic change is intended.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: The destination variable's type controls how the entire right-hand expression is evaluated from the beginning.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Conversions and numeric boundaries: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Signed-to-unsigned conversion is modulo the unsigned range, narrowing can lose information, and out-of-range floating or pointer conversions have separate rules.
